subject: Physics
unit: Unit 6: Mechanical Oscillation and Sound Wave

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 6: Mechanical Oscillation and Sound Wave
questions.question: What characteristic of a sound wave determines its pitch?
questions.explanation: Pitch is determined by frequency: higher frequency gives higher pitch. Amplitude determines loudness, not pitch.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: Mechanical Oscillation and Sound Wave
source_trace.content_anchor: Pitch depends on frequency: higher frequency gives higher pitch.

explanations_tiered.definition: Pitch is the perceived highness or lowness of a sound, determined by frequency.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Higher frequency = higher pitch (e.g., a whistle). Lower frequency = lower pitch (e.g., a drum).
explanations_tiered.beginner: A small bell rings with a high pitch because it vibrates quickly (high frequency).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Pitch correlates with frequency, measured in Hertz. Humans hear roughly 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Pitch perception is logarithmic; doubling frequency raises pitch by one octave.
reasoning_steps: Recall which wave property controls pitch.
misconceptions: Some think loudness affects pitch; they are independent. A loud sound can be low or high pitch.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Pitch ↔ Frequency. High f = high pitch.

glossary_entry.definition: The perceived highness or lowness of a sound, determined by frequency.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Perceived highness/lowness of sound, determined by frequency.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Play different frequency tones on a computer to demonstrate.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Pitch depends on frequency; amplitude affects loudness.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 6: Mechanical Oscillation and Sound Wave
questions.question: What wave property determines the loudness of a sound?
questions.explanation: Loudness is related to amplitude: larger amplitude means louder sound. Frequency determines pitch, not loudness.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: Mechanical Oscillation and Sound Wave
source_trace.content_anchor: Loudness depends on amplitude: larger amplitude = louder sound.

explanations_tiered.definition: Loudness is the perceived intensity of a sound, determined by amplitude.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Greater amplitude means more energy and a louder sound. Measured in decibels (dB).
explanations_tiered.beginner: A big drum hit moves farther (big amplitude) and sounds louder than a soft tap.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Amplitude corresponds to pressure variation; larger pressure changes are perceived as louder.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Intensity \(I \propto A^2\). Doubling amplitude quadruples intensity, adding ~6 dB.
misconceptions: Some confuse loudness with pitch; loudness is amplitude, pitch is frequency.

glossary_entry.definition: The perceived intensity of a sound, related to amplitude.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Perceived intensity of sound, determined by amplitude.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show waveform with different amplitudes on oscilloscope.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Loudness depends on amplitude, not frequency.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 6: Mechanical Oscillation and Sound Wave
questions.question: Why does the same note sound different when played on a violin versus a piano?
questions.hint: It's the quality or colour of the sound.
questions.explanation: Timber (quality) distinguishes sounds of same pitch and loudness from different sources, due to different harmonic content. Amplitude and frequency alone don't explain the difference.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: Mechanical Oscillation and Sound Wave
source_trace.content_anchor: Timber is the quality that distinguishes tones of same pitch and intensity from different sources.

explanations_tiered.definition: Timber (or quality) is the characteristic that allows distinction between sounds of same pitch and loudness from different sources.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Different instruments produce different harmonic overtones, giving unique waveforms even at the same fundamental frequency.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A violin and a piano playing the same note sound different because of their unique 'voice' — that's timber.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Timber depends on the relative intensities of harmonics (overtones) in the sound wave.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Fourier analysis shows each instrument has a unique harmonic spectrum, giving distinct waveform shape.
reasoning_steps: Identify it as the quality difference.
Select timber as the correct term.
misconceptions: Some think frequency or amplitude changes; same note means same fundamental frequency and similar loudness, so timber must differ.

glossary_entry.definition: The quality of a sound that distinguishes it from another of the same pitch and loudness.

flashcard.front: Why do violin and piano playing same note sound different?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What makes same note sound different on different instruments?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Quality that distinguishes sounds of same pitch and loudness from different sources.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Play same note on different instruments (e.g., piano vs violin) and ask students to identify difference.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Timber (quality) differs; pitch and loudness can be identical but sound different due to harmonics.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 6: Mechanical Oscillation and Sound Wave
questions.question: Two waves with amplitudes of \(3\,\text{cm}\) and \(4\,\text{cm}\) meet in phase. What is the amplitude of the resulting wave?
questions.explanation: In-phase superposition is constructive: amplitudes add: \(3 + 4 = 7\,\text{cm}\). Option B (5 cm) would be magnitude of difference for destructive.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: Mechanical Oscillation and Sound Wave
source_trace.content_anchor: When waves are in phase, they interfere constructively, amplitudes add.

explanations_tiered.definition: Constructive interference occurs when waves meet in phase, resulting in amplitude equal to the sum of individual amplitudes.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: In phase: crest meets crest; displacements add. Out of phase: crest meets trough; they subtract (destructive).
explanations_tiered.beginner: If two pushes both go up together, the water goes up higher — that's addition.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For identical frequency waves in phase, resultant amplitude \(A = A_1 + A_2\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify condition: in phase = constructive.
misconceptions: Some subtract in phase or add out of phase; in phase = sum, out of phase = difference.

glossary_entry.definition: Superposition of waves in phase, giving amplitude sum.

flashcard.front: 3 cm and 4 cm waves in phase: resultant amplitude?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: 3 cm and 4 cm waves in phase: amplitude?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: In-phase superposition resulting in amplitude sum.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use water wave analogy: two peaks meet = bigger peak.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Constructive: 3+4=7 cm. Option B (5 cm) is for destructive (|3-4|=1? Actually 1, not 5 — other wrong distractors).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 6: Mechanical Oscillation and Sound Wave
questions.question: Two identical waves of amplitude \(5\,\text{cm}\) meet exactly out of phase. What is the amplitude of the resultant wave?
questions.hint: Out of phase = crest meets trough.
questions.explanation: Destructive interference: when amplitudes are equal and opposite, they cancel completely, giving zero amplitude. Option C would be constructive.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: Mechanical Oscillation and Sound Wave
source_trace.content_anchor: Out of phase waves interfere destructively; equal amplitudes cancel.

explanations_tiered.definition: Destructive interference occurs when waves are out of phase, resulting in amplitude equal to the absolute difference of individual amplitudes.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Out of phase: crest meets trough. For equal amplitudes, they cancel completely (zero amplitude).
explanations_tiered.beginner: If one push goes up and another equal push goes down at the same spot, the water stays flat — cancellation.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For waves exactly opposite (\(180^\circ\) out of phase), resultant amplitude \(A = |A_1 - A_2|\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Destructive interference leads to intensity minima; for identical waves, energy is redistributed to constructive regions.
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify condition: out of phase (destructive).
reasoning_steps: Determine interference type (out of phase → destructive).
misconceptions: Some add out of phase; they should subtract. Equal amplitudes cancel to zero.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Destructive: out of phase, subtract amplitudes.

glossary_entry.definition: Superposition of waves out of phase, giving amplitude difference.

flashcard.front: Two 5 cm waves out of phase: resultant amplitude?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Two 5 cm waves out of phase: amplitude?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show with slinky: sending a pulse while another pulse arrives inverted cancels.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Equal amplitudes out of phase cancel to zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 6: Mechanical Oscillation and Sound Wave
questions.question: Sound becomes harmful and may cause hearing loss when its level exceeds approximately:
questions.hint: Prolonged exposure above 75 dB can be harmful.
questions.explanation: Noise becomes harmful above 75 dB (prolonged exposure) and painful above 120 dB. 50 dB is quiet conversation; 30 dB is whisper.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: Mechanical Oscillation and Sound Wave
source_trace.content_anchor: Noise becomes harmful when it exceeds 75 dB and painful above 120 dB.

explanations_tiered.definition: Sound pollution is excessive noise that can cause adverse health effects, beginning above 75 dB.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Prolonged exposure above 75 dB can lead to hearing loss; above 120 dB is painful and causes immediate damage.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A vacuum cleaner (~75 dB) can harm ears over long time; a rock concert (~120 dB) hurts immediately.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Decibel scale is logarithmic; 75 dB is about 10 times louder than 65 dB. Safe exposure time decreases rapidly above 75 dB.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Hearing damage risk depends on both intensity and duration. NIOSH recommends exposure limits: 85 dB for 8 hours, with halving time per +3 dB.
reasoning_steps: Select 75 dB as lower harmful limit.
misconceptions: Some think only very loud sounds (>120 dB) are harmful; prolonged 75 dB also damages hearing.

glossary_entry.definition: Excessive noise that may cause adverse health effects.

flashcard.front: Above what dB does sound become harmful?

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Discuss common sources: traffic (~85 dB), headphones at max (~100 dB).
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. 75 dB is harmful prolonged; 120 dB painful.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 6: Mechanical Oscillation and Sound Wave
questions.question: A pendulum of length L has period T. What would be the period if the length is quadrupled?
questions.explanation: Period \(T = 2\pi\sqrt{L/g}\). If length quadruples, \(\sqrt{4L} = 2\sqrt{L}\), so period doubles to \(2T\). Option C confuses proportional vs square root.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: Mechanical Oscillation and Sound Wave

explanations_tiered.definition: Pendulum period depends on square root of length: \(T = 2\pi\sqrt{L/g}\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Quadrupling length doubles period because \(\sqrt{4} = 2\). Mass and amplitude (small) do not affect period.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you make the string four times longer, the swing takes twice as long for each back-and-forth.
misconceptions: Common error: period proportional to L (instead of √L), leading to quadruple = 4T.

glossary_entry.definition: Period is proportional to square root of length.

misconceptions: Some think length quadruples → period quadruples. Incorrect; it's square root.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Time for one swing, proportional to √L.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 6: Mechanical Oscillation and Sound Wave
questions.question: In a standing wave, points with zero displacement are called:
questions.hint: Nodes are stationary points of no motion.
questions.explanation: Nodes are fixed points with zero displacement; antinodes have maximum displacement. Crests/troughs are for traveling waves.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: Mechanical Oscillation and Sound Wave
source_trace.content_anchor: Nodes are positions with zero displacement; antinodes are maximum displacement.

explanations_tiered.definition: Nodes are points in a standing wave that remain at rest (zero displacement).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Nodes occur where destructive interference always happens. Antinodes are where constructive interference gives maximum motion.
explanations_tiered.beginner: On a vibrating guitar string, the points that don't move (like the ends) are nodes.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For a standing wave \(y = 2A\sin(kx)\cos(\omega t)\), nodes occur when \(\sin(kx)=0\), i.e., \(x = n\lambda/2\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Nodes are separated by \(\lambda/2\); distance from node to adjacent antinode is \(\lambda/4\).
misconceptions: Some confuse nodes and antinodes: nodes = zero motion, antinodes = maximum motion.

glossary_entry.definition: Point in a standing wave with zero displacement.

flashcard.front: What are points of zero displacement in a standing wave?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Zero displacement points in standing wave?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Point of zero displacement in a standing wave.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Draw standing wave and label nodes/antinodes.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Nodes = zero displacement; antinodes = maximum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 6: Mechanical Oscillation and Sound Wave
questions.structural_anchor: Speed of sound in different media
questions.question: Why does sound travel faster in solids than in gases?

options.B: Particles in solids are closer and more elastic

questions.hint: Closer particles mean faster energy transfer.
questions.explanation: In solids, particles are tightly packed and strongly bonded, allowing quicker transfer of vibrations. Option A (heavier) is false; density alone doesn't increase speed.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: Mechanical Oscillation and Sound Wave
source_trace.structural_element: Speed of sound in different media
source_trace.content_anchor: Particles in solids are close so they easily pass sound; sound travels faster in solids.

explanations_tiered.definition: Sound speed depends on medium elasticity and density; solids have higher elasticity and closer particles, increasing speed.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Sound is a mechanical wave; closer particle spacing and stronger interparticle forces allow faster vibration transfer.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Whisper in a solid wall: vibrations pass quickly because particles are tightly packed.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Speed in solids: \(v = \sqrt{Y/\rho}\) (Young's modulus), much larger than \(v = \sqrt{B/\rho}\) in gases due to higher elasticity.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Young's modulus for steel is ~200 GPa; bulk modulus of air ~0.1 MPa, hence v_steel ~5000 m/s vs v_air ~340 m/s.
reasoning_steps: Recall factor: particle closeness and elasticity.
Identify correct option describing close particles.
misconceptions: Some think heavier = faster; but steel is denser than air yet faster, but within solids, higher density can reduce speed if elasticity doesn't compensate.

glossary_entry.definition: Faster than in gases due to higher elasticity and closer particle spacing.

flashcard.front: Why faster sound in solids than gases?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Particles closer and more elastic, transferring vibrations quicker.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why does sound travel faster in solids?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Particles are closer and more elastic, transferring vibrations faster.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Higher than in gases due to particle proximity and elasticity.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Tap a table and listen through air vs ear on table.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Compare speed of sound in different materials
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Close particle spacing and elasticity enable faster transfer; heaviness (A) is not correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 6: Mechanical Oscillation and Sound Wave
questions.question: A student thinks that doubling the mass attached to a spring will double the period. Is this correct?
questions.explanation: Period \(T = 2\pi\sqrt{m/k}\) shows T ∝ √m, not m. Doubling mass multiplies period by √2 ≈ 1.41, not 2. Option A is the misconception.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: Mechanical Oscillation and Sound Wave

explanations_tiered.definition: Spring-mass period \(T = 2\pi\sqrt{m/k}\): period is proportional to square root of mass.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Doubling mass multiplies period by √2 ≈ 1.41, not 2. The misconception is thinking direct proportion.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you double the weight on a spring, it doesn't take twice as long to bounce; it takes about 1.4 times as long.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: T ∝ √m. Doubling m → new period = T × √2 ≈ 1.41T.
explanations_tiered.advanced: From energy or differential equation: \(\ddot{x} + (k/m)x = 0\) gives ω = √(k/m), T = 2π/ω = 2π√(m/k).
misconceptions: Students often incorrectly think T ∝ m (direct) instead of T ∝ √m.

glossary_entry.definition: Period is proportional to square root of mass.

flashcard.front: Double mass on spring: period doubles?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Double mass on spring: period doubles? Why/why not?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Period is proportional to square root of mass.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show with two masses: m and 2m, measure period ratio ≈ 1.41.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. T ∝ √m, so doubling m increases T by √2, not 2.
